original: Saint Boniface I ends his reign as Catholic Pope
Shimin becomes Emperor Taizong of Tang, Emperor of China, when his father abdicates in his favour
Aethelstan crowned King of the Anglo Saxons by the Archbishop of Canterbury at Kingston upon Thames
Battle at Montaperti, Tuscany, between rival factions the Guelphs and Ghibellines
King of Aragon Pedro III annexes Sicily
Catholic rebellion in Scotland
"Rodi" avalanche destroys Plurs, Switzerland, and kills 1500
The first opera staged in Vilnius and in the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, "The Abduction of Helen," debuts in the Lower Castle of Vilnius
City of Amsterdam signs a trade agreement with American rebels
Los Angeles is founded by 44 Spanish-speaking mestizos in the Bahía de las Fumas (Bay of Smokes)
William of Orange's troops overthrow the Dutch towns of Hattem and Elburg
The first edition of the Batavian State Courier is published
The Religious Remembrancer, the first US religious newspaper later renamed The Christian Observer, is published by the Presbyterian publishing center
Ten-year-old Barney Flaherty becomes the first newsboy hired by the New York Sun
Work on Cologne Cathedral recommences after a 284-year hiatus
English and French lay siege to the eastern Russian city of Petropavlovsk-Kamchatsky during the Crimean War
North Beach & Mission Railway Company organized in San Francisco
Bread riots in Mobile, Alabama
Britain ends its policy of penal transportation to New South Wales in Australia
The first cafeteria-style, self-service restaurant, the Exchange Buffet, opens in New York City
In New York City, 12,000 tailors go on strike to protest against sweatshops
An 8.3-magnitude earthquake shakes Yakutat Bay, Alaska
Dalai Lama signs treaty allowing British commerce in Tibet
New York Highlanders beat Boston Pilgrims 7-0 and 1-0 for their MLB record fifth consecutive doubleheader sweep
French aviator Roland Garros sets a world altitude record of 4,250 m (13,944 ft)
First accident (collision) in London Underground: 22 people injured
France, Russia, and Britain agree in the Pact of London that none will make a separate peace
General von Moltke ceases German advance in France
Jhr. Ch. Ruys de Beerenbrouck becomes the first Dutch Catholic prime minister
US troops land in Arkhangelsk, Russia, and stay for 10 months
The British intervene in Petrograd
Last day of the Julian civil calendar in parts of Bulgaria
Man o' War wins the 1 5/8-mile Lawrence Realization Stakes at Belmont Park by 100 lengths, the largest winning margin in modern thoroughbred racing history, with a world record time of 2:40.8
Maiden flight of the first US airship, USS Shenandoah
New York Yankees pitcher "Sad" Sam Jones no-hits Philadelphia A's, 2-0
Cambridge Theatre opens in London
First airplane to exceed 300 mph (483 kph), J.R. Wendell, Glenview, IL

British-Kenyan aviator Beryl Markham takes off from Abingdon in England on a solo flight across the Atlantic Ocean and crash-lands at Baleine Cove on Cape Breton Island, Nova Scotia, Canada, becoming the first woman to make the east-west trip
Largo Caballero becomes Spanish prime minister
Fifteen-year-old cyclist Doris Kopsky becomes the first Amateur Bicycle League of America women's national champion in 4:22.4 in Buffalo, New York
Dutch Second Chamber affirms the Netherlands is in a state of war with Germany
German troops move into Gdańsk
Mir, a Nazi ghetto in occupied Poland, is exterminated
Netherlands and Belgium declare neutrality
RAF bombs Wilhelmshaven, Germany
CBS begins broadcasting TV as station W2XAB in New York City
New York Yankees clinch their third straight AL pennant, beating the Red Sox 6-3; the earliest date in baseball history a team captures a pennant
US destroyer Greer fires at German submarine U-652
Transport #28 departs with French Jews to Nazi Germany
British 8th Army lands in Taranto, South Italy
2,087 Jews are transported from Westerbork to KZ-Lower Theresienstadt
British 11th Armoured Division liberates Antwerp, Belgium
Finland breaks diplomatic contact with Nazi Germany
US 1st Army liberates Namur, Belgium
American chess grandmaster Reuben Fine wins four simultaneous rapid (10 seconds per move) games blindfolded in New York City
US regains possession of Wake Island from Japan
Champion Middlesex cricket batsman Denis Compton scores 101 for South of England in a tour match defeat to South Africa at Lord's, achieving his record 17th century of the English season
Marie Robie sinks a 393-yard (359.36 m) hole-in-one on the first hole at Furnace Brook
"Beetle Bailey" comic strip debuts in 12 newspapers
D. McI. Hodgson of St. Ann's Bay, Nova Scotia, catches a 977 lb (428.2 kg) tuna, the heaviest tuna ever taken on a rod and reel
First helicopter rescue of an American pilot from behind enemy lines during the Korean War
Heavy typhoon strikes Japan, killing about 250
NASCAR's first paved superspeedway, Darlington Raceway, hosts the Southern 500, the first 500-mile event in NASCAR history; Johnny Mantz wins in a Plymouth
First transcontinental TV broadcast by US President Harry Truman
NBC extends to become a 61-station coast-to-coast network
WATR (now WTXX) TV channel 20 in Waterbury, CT (NBC) begins broadcasting
WGEM TV channel 10 in Quincy-Hannibal, IL (NBC) begins broadcasting
Peter B. Cortese of the US achieves a one-arm deadlift of 370 lbs, 22 lbs over triple his body weight, in York, Pennsylvania
Ford Motor Co. introduces the Edsel automobile range
Hurricane Donna kills 148 in the Caribbean and US
US authorizes Agency for International Development
The Beatles record "How Do You Do It" at EMI Studios in London
Swissair Flight 306 crashes near Dürrenäsch, Switzerland, killing all 80 people on board
Eduardo Frei Montalva is elected President of Chile
NASA launches its first Orbital Geophysical Observatory (OGO-1)
Scottish Forth Road Bridge opens as the longest in Europe at the time
KREZ TV channel 6 in Durango, CO (CBS/NBC) begins broadcasting
Rock group The Who's van is vandalized with $10,000 worth of equipment stolen while the group is inside Battersea Dogs Home buying a guard dog at the time
The Beatles' single "Help!" goes to #1 and stays #1 for three weeks
Australian Jack Brabham clinches his third Formula 1 World Drivers' Championship with a sixth place in the Italian Grand Prix at Monza
Jerry Lewis's second Muscular Dystrophy telethon
Train crash in Arnhem, Netherlands, kills 5
Nigerian troops conquer the city of Aba, Biafra, during the Nigerian Civil War
29.0 cm of rainfall at Workman Creek, Arizona (state record)
An Irish Republican Army (IRA) member, Michael Kane (35), is killed in the premature explosion of the bomb he was planting at an electricity transformer in Belfast
Russian ballerina Natalia Makarova defects to the West while on tour with the Kirov Ballet in London and is granted political asylum
Alaskan 727 crashes into Chilkoot Mountain, Alaska, killing 109
"The Price is Right," the US's longest-running games show, debuts on CBS

16-year-old Ulrike Meyfarth of West Germany, with a world record-equalling 1.92 m, wins the women's high jump gold medal at the Munich Olympics; she is the youngest winner of an individual athletics event in Olympic history

American swimmer Melissa Belote, with a world record of 2:19.19, wins the 200 m backstroke gold medal at the Munich Olympics and wraps up the backstroke double, having won the 100 m in Olympic record time
American swimmer Mike Burton sets a world record of 15:52.58 to win the 1500 m gold medal at the Munich Olympics
Italian diver Klaus Dibiasi wins his second of three consecutive 10 m platform gold medals at the Munich Olympics
Kenyan legend Kipchoge Keino follows his 1500 m gold in Mexico City with an Olympic record of 8:23.64 to win the 3000 m steeplechase at the Munich Olympics
Soviet sprinter Valeriy Borzov runs 20.00 to win the men's 200 m gold medal at the Munich Olympics, wrapping up the 100/200 m double at the Games
Thieves steal 18 paintings from the Montreal Museum of Fine Arts, the largest art theft in North America at the time
USSR performs an underground nuclear test
Willem Ruska of the Netherlands wins gold medals in both the heavyweight and open class Olympic judo competitions in Munich, becoming the first judoka to win two Olympic gold medals at one Games
William E. Colby becomes the 10th director of the CIA
Egypt and Israel sign the Sinai Interim Agreement (also known as the Sinai II Agreement) with the intention of peacefully resolving territorial disputes in Geneva, Switzerland
Palestinians hijack KLM DC-9 to Cyprus
Jerry Lewis's 13th Muscular Dystrophy telethon raises $29,074,405
India needs 438 to win against England; game ends at 8-429
Iranian army conquers Baneh
Progressive rock group Yes perform their last concert at Madison Square Garden, New York City
Longest MLB game at Fenway Park ends in 20 innings as Seattle Mariners beat Boston Red Sox 8-7
NewscasterDavid Brinkleyis released by NBC
US performs a nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Arson fire engulfs an apartment-hotel in Los Angeles, killing 25
USSR performs an underground nuclear test
Californian skier Scott Michael Pellaton sets barefoot waterski speed record at 119.36 mph (191.51 km/h)
Greg LeMondwins UCI World Road Race Championship in 7h 01' 21" in Altenrhein, Switzerland; first American cyclist to take the title
US Men's Amateur Golf Championship, North Shore CC: Jay Sigel successfully defends his title 8 and 7 over Chris Perry
Nigerian singerFela Kutiis sentenced to 2 years on politically motivated charges of currency smuggling
New York Mets catcherGary Carterhits two home runs in a 9-2 win in San Diego, tying the MLB record of five home runs in two games
Russian Igor Paklin sets a new high jump world record at 2.41 m in Kobe, Japan
Mike Tysoncrashes a silver BMW into a tree near Catskills, New York
The relocated Phoenix Cardinals play their first regular-season NFL game and lose 21-14 to the Bengals at Riverfront Stadium in Cincinnati
Jerry Lewis's 24th Muscular Dystrophy telethon raises $42,737,219
Monday demonstrations demanding political reforms begin at St. Nicholas Church in Leipzig, East Germany, and are later replicated around the country
MLB Statistical Accuracy Committee votes to drop the asterisk next toRoger Maris' 1961 home run record of 61, passing Babe Ruth's 60, and determines an official no-hitter must go at least 9 innings
Route 35 Theater in Hazlet closes its doors, the last drive-in in New Jersey
"Scared Silent" about child abuse and hosted by Oprah is the first non-news program to air on three networks simultaneously (CBS, NBC, and PBS)
MLB New York Yankees one-handed pitcher Jim Abbott no-hits Cleveland Indians 4-0 at Yankee Stadium
Penn State University plays its first football game in the Big Ten, beating Minnesota 38-20; quarterback John Sacca throws four touchdown passes to Bobby Engram
Bulgarian government of Lyuben Berov falls
Chinese diver Fu Mingxia wins the 10 m platform gold medal at the World Swimming Championships in Rome, Italy, becoming the first female to win back-to-back world titles in the event
Kansai International airport officially opens near Osaka, Japan
Miami quarterbackDan Marinothrows five touchdown passes in Dolphins' 39-35 win over New England; sets NFL record with his 18th game of four or more touchdown passes
Tom Tupa scores the first two-point conversion in NFL history, running a fake extra point attempt for the Cleveland Browns in a 28-20 win at Cincinnati
Chicago White Sox third baseman Robin Ventura hits grand slams in consecutive innings, the fourth and fifth, of a 14-3 rout of the Texas Rangers
Fourth World Conference on Women opens in Beijing with over 4,750 delegates from 181 countries in attendance
Jerry Lewis's 30th Muscular Dystrophy telethon raises $47,800,000
Howard Stern Radio Show premieres in Louisville, Kentucky, on WTFX 100.5 FM
First "Who Wants to Be a Millionaire?" hosted by Chris Tarrant debuts on ITV in Britain
NY Yankees beat Chicago White Sox, 11-6, to win their 100th game on the earliest date in MLB history besting the 1906 Chicago Cubs and 1954 Cleveland Indians by 5 days
MLB's Cincinnati Reds set a National League record with nine home runs as they rout the Philadelphia Phillies 22-3
After blowing an 11-run lead to Kansas City, Scott Hatteberg's bottom of the 9th walk-off home run gives Oakland a historic MLB victory, 12-11; A's set an AL record 20-game winning streak

Argentina pulls off one of the biggest upsets in basketball history, beating the US 87-80 in the second round of the World Championships; first US loss in 59 games since sending NBA players to international competition in 1992

Damon Allen moves past former CFL and NFL star Warren Moon as professional football's all-time leading passer, throwing for 207 yards and two scores as Toronto routs Hamilton 40-6 to achieve 70,596 career passing yards
A 7.1 magnitude earthquake strikes the South Island of New Zealand at 4:35 am, causing widespread damage and several power outages in the Canterbury region
25 people are killed in a funeral suicide bombing in Nangarhar, Pakistan
42 people are killed and 25 injured as a passenger bus falls into a ravine in Morocco
A "magic carpet" that can help prevent falls among the elderly by warning them of unusual footsteps is developed by researchers at the University of Manchester
Pauline Marois becomes the first female premier of Quebec
Archaeological remains of a Viking fortress from the 900s CE, Vallø Borgring, are discovered in Denmark
Monsoon rains begin and cause flooding over a sustained period, killing over 400 people in India and Pakistan
400-year-old sunken Portuguese spice trade ship discovered near the port of Cascais, Portugal
Amazon becomes America's second trillion-dollar company
Italian city Florence bans people from eating on the street in its historic center
NFL playerColin Kaepernick, who kneeled during the US national anthem, is the new face of Nike's 30th anniversary advertising campaign
Strongest typhoon to hit Japan in 25 years, Jebi hits Tokushima Prefecture, swamping Kansai Airport and killing at least 11
WHO reports that more than a quarter of people worldwide (1.4 billion) do not get enough physical exercise to avoid major diseases
Hong Kong leader Carrie Lam withdraws the controversial extradition bill to China after three months of massive protests
A record 52% of American 18-29-year-olds live with their parents due to the pandemic, according to a Pew Research Center study
Mass stabbing leaves 10 people dead and 19 injured with two suspects on the run in James Smith Cree Nation in the Canadian province of Saskatchewan
N. K. Jemisin wins her fifth Hugo Award for her graphic novel Far Sector (Best Graphic Story or Comic), making her one of the few authors to achieve this
Lagos' first-ever metro rail service, the Blue Line, begins operation in Nigeria, reducing a 2-hour journey to 15 minutes
Military head General Brice Clotaire Oligui Nguema is sworn in as Gabon's Head of State a week after ousting President Ali Bongo Ondimba, whose family has ruled for more than 50 years
Novo Nordisk, Danish manufacturer of weight-loss drug Wegovy, becomes Europe's most valuable company at $428 billion (£339 billion), overtaking France's LVMH
Storm Daniel begins across Greece, Turkey, and Bulgaria, killing more than a dozen people as some regions receive 800 mm (31.5 inches) of rain, more than a year's worth of precipitation
14-year-old US student kills two teachers and two students, hospitalizing nine, at Apalachee High School, Winder, Georgia
Joseph McNeil, American USAF Major-General, and civil rights advocate (Greensboro Four lunch counter sit-in, 1960), dies of complications of Parkinson's disease at 83

Long-lost painting, 'Portrait of a Lady' by Giuseppe Ghislandi, is handed over to the Argentine justice system after being discovered in an estate that belonged to the daughter of a Nazi fugitive; the painting was looted in Nazi-occupied Europe, missing for 80 years
Treaty of Alcáçovas-Toledo: King Alfonso V of Portugal recognizesIsabella Ias Queen of Castile, the first treaty to recognize areas of influence outside Europe, with Portugal gaining the Atlantic
NavigatorHenry Hudsonis the first European to discover the island of Manhattan [or September 11]
KingCharles IIof England, on the run from Parliamentarians at White Ladies Priory, disguises himself as a countryman by rubbing his hands and face with soot
French garrison of Castle Namur surrenders to the army of the Grand Alliance under the command of KingWilliam IIIof England
Robert Fultonbegins operating scheduled passenger service on his steamboat between New York and Albany
Chilean independence figureJosé Miguel Carrerais shot and killed after a show trial in Mendoza, Argentina

British Association for the Advancement of Science proposes standard units of electrical measurement (the Volt afterAlessandro Volta; the Ohm afterGeorg Ohm) at its 31st annual meeting (Manchester, England)
General Leebegins the Maryland Campaign, invading the North with 50,000 Confederate troops (U.S. Civil War)
The Third French Republic is proclaimed as France overthrows EmperorNapoleon III, who ironically is the elected president of the Second French Republic, after his defeat by Prussia at Sedan
In its first large-scale test,Thomas Edison's light bulb is used to light New York's Pearl Street Station
Apache ChiefGeronimosurrenders, ending the last major US-Indian war
English authorBeatrix Potterwrites the story of Peter Rabbit for a 5-year-old boy

Future Baseball Hall of Fame pitchersChristy Mathewsonand Mordecai Brown face each other for the final time; after 25 contests, Mathewson takes a 13-12 advantage with the Reds as 10-8 winners over the Cubs
Mustafa Kemal Atatürk, who founded the Republic of Turkey, gathers a congress in Sivas to make decisions as to the future of Anatolia and Thrace
Finnish super athletePaavo Nurmiruns a 2000 m world record of 5:26.3 in Tampere, Finland
William Walmsley andWilliam Lyonsofficially found the Swallow Sidecar Company (later Jaguar Cars) in Blackpool, England
André Charlot and Noel Coward's revue "London Calling" premieres in London
Charles Lindberghvisits Boise, Idaho, on his cross-country tour
Coup against Cuban President Carlos Manuel de Céspedes y Quesada byFulgencio Batista
RKO Radio Pictures releases "Swing Time," directed by George Stevens and starringFred AstaireandGinger Rogers
SurgeonArchibald McIndoesets up a new Centre for Plastic and Jaw Surgery at Queen Victoria Hospital, East Grinstead, England, to treat injured airmen
Nazi collaborator Anton Mussert puts the fate of the Netherlands inHitler's hands
Queen Wilhelminaabdicates the Dutch throne in favor of her daughter Juliana due to illness after 58 years, the longest reign of any Dutch monarch
US PresidentHarry Trumanaddresses the opening of the Japanese Peace Treaty Conference
Carol Burnett-Richard Hayes Show premieres on CBS Radio
French PresidentCharles de Gaullevisits West Germany
MarxistSalvador Allendewins a narrow plurality of votes in Chile's presidential election
John LennonandYoko Onoperform three songs ("Imagine," "Now or Never," "Give Peace a Chance") atJerry Lewis's 7th Muscular Dystrophy Telethon at the Americana Hotel, New York City

US 4 x 100 m medley relay team of Mike Stamm, Tom Bruce,Mark Spitz, and Jerry Heidenreich sets a world record of 3:48.16 to win gold at the Munich Olympics; Mark Spitz becomes the first athlete to win seven Olympic gold medals at a single Games
Revival ofFrank Loesser's 1956 musical "The Most Happy Fella" opens at the NY State Theater in New York City for a limited run by the New York City Opera
SingerHarry Nilssonmakes a rare and final concert appearance, joining Ringo and His All-Starr Band for a version of his "Without You" at Caesars Palace, Las Vegas, Nevada
Cult TV series "Xena: Warrior Princess," starringLucy Lawlessand Renee O'Connor, debuts in the US
Google is formally incorporated byLarry PageandSergey Brin, two students at Stanford University
Before a U.S. Congressional panel,Doris Robertstestifies that age discrimination is prevalent in Hollywood
Kelly Clarksonis crowned the winner of the first season of the "American Idol" TV singing competition series
20-year-oldKyle Buschbecomes the youngest driver to win a NASCAR Cup Series race when he outduels Greg Biffle in the Sony HD 500 at California Speedway
Mother Teresais canonized byPope Francisin a ceremony at the Vatican
Nobel Peace laureateMalala YousafzaicriticizesAung San Suu Kyi's lack of response to the Rohingya crisis in Myanmar
US PresidentDonald Trumpannounces the Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals (DACA) program will be stopped
British rebel alliance of MPs votes to ban a no-deal Brexit and rejects PMBoris Johnson's call for a snap election
YouTube is fined $170 million for illegally collecting data on children's viewing habits by the US Federal Trade Commission
Nigerian government announces it is suspending Twitter indefinitely after removing a post by PresidentMuhammadu Buhari
Romulus Augustulus, the last Western Roman Emperor, abdicates after forces led by Odoacer invade Rome, the traditional date for the end of the Western Roman Empire
George Eastmanpatents the first roll-film camera and registers the brand name "Kodak"
Governor of Arkansas, Orval Faubus, calls out the National Guard to prevent nine Black students from entering Little Rock Central High School
FBI announces it has recovered a pair of Dorothy's ruby slippers from "The Wizard of Oz," stolen 13 years earlier
Al-Biruni, Iranian mathematician, astronomer and polymath, born in Kath, Khwarezm, Afrighid dynasty (d. 1048) [some sources cite 5th as birth date]
Alexander III, King of Scotland, born in Roxburgh Castle, Roxburghshire (d. 1286)
Amadeus V de Great, Count of Flanders and Savoy, born in Le Bourget-du-Lac, France (d. 1323)
Amadeus VIII, Duke of Savoy and last antipope (Felix V, 1439-48), born in Chambéry, France (d. 1451)
Henry Stafford, 2nd Duke of Buckingham, English politician, born in Wales (d. 1483)
Sophie of Mecklenburg-Güstrow, Queen of Denmark and Norway, born in Wismar, Germany (d. 1631)
Wanli, Emperor of Ming Dynasty, born in China (d. 1620)
Constantijn Huygens, Dutch diplomat and poet, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1687)
Jacob Hintze, German composer, born in Bernau, Electorate of Brandenburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1702)
Gerhard Noodt, Dutch lawyer, born in Nijemegen, Netherlands (d. 1725)

Job Orton, English minister, born in Shrewsbury, England (d. 1783)
Bernardus Bosch, Dutch vicar and poet, born in Deventer, Netherlands (d. 1803)

François René de Chateaubriand, French novelist (Atala), born in Saint-Malo, France (d. 1848)

Friedrich August von Alberti, German geologist (Triasic Age), born in Stuttgart, Germany (d. 1878)

Donald McKay, Canadian-American naval architect (built fastest clipper ships), born in Nova Scotia, Canada (d. 1880)
Andreas Ignatius Schaepman, Archbishop of Utrecht (1868-82), born in Zwolle, Netherlands (d. 1882)
Mihály Mosonyi [Michael Brand], Hungarian composer, born in Boldogasszony,  Kingdom of Hungary (d. 1870)
François Bazin, French composer, born in Marseille, France (d. 1878)
Phoebe Cary, American poet (Poems of Alice & Phoebe Cary), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1871)
Dadabhai Naoroji, 1st Indian in British parliament, born in Mumbai, India (d. 1917)
Martin Wiberg, Swedish computer pioneer, born in Viby, Scania, Sweden (d. 1905)
Antonio Agliardi, Italian diplomat and Roman Catholic cardinal, born in Cologno al Serio, Italy (d. 1915)
Charles Dilke, British statesman and Radical politician, born in London (d. 1911)
Ján Levoslav Bella, Slovak composer, born in Liptovský Mikuláš, Austrian Empire (d. 1936)
Daniel Burnham, American architect and urban designer (built skyscrapers), born in Henderson, New York (d. 1912)

Lewis Howard Latimer, American inventor (carbon filament lightbulbs), born in Chelsea, Massachusetts (d. 1928)
Luigi Cadorna, Italian fieldmarshal (WW I-Caporetto), born in Verbania, Kingdom of Sardinia (d. 1928)
John Dillon, Irish nationalist and British Lower house leader, born in Blackrock, Dublin (d. 1927)
Hermann Wissmann, German African explorer and governor of East-Africa, born in Frankfurt, Germany (d. 1905)
Edoardo Mascheroni, Italian composer, born in Milan, Kingdom of Sardinia (d. 1941)

Karl Seitz, President of Austria (1920), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1950)

[Rosalie] Julia Cuypers, Flemish actress and wife of Joseph of Lyre, born in St-Jans-Molenbeek, Belgium (d. 1952)

Antonio Bacci, Italian cardinal, born in Giugnola, Italy (d. 1971)
Albert Orsborn, 6th General of The Salvation Army, born in Clapton, England (d. 1967)
Oskar Schlemmer, German painter, sculptor and choreographer (Triadisches Ballett), born in Stuttgart, German Empire (d. 1943)
Sam Lanin, American sweet jazz arranger and bandleader (Roseland Orchestra; The Ipana Troubadors), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1977)
Henry Schultz, American economist (econometrics), born in Sharkawshchyna, Russian Empire (d. 1938)
Antonin Artaud, French songwriter and director (Theatre Seraphin), born in Marseille, France (d. 1948)

Paul Osborn, American playwright (Mornings at 7), born in Evansville, Indiana (d. 1988)
Tommy Mitchell, English cricket spin bowler (5 Tests, 8 wickets; Derbyshire CCC), born in Cresswell, England (d. 1996)
Christian-Jaque [Christian Maudet], French film director (Fearless Little Soldier, Race for Life, The Pearls of the Crown), born in Paris (d. 1994)
Julian W. Hill, American research chemist (developed nylon), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1996)
Max Delbrück, German-American biologist and molecular geneticist (bacteriophage, Nobel Prize 1969), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1981)
Sabin Carr, American athlete (Olympic gold pole vault 1928; WR 4.27m 1927), born in Santa Barbara, California (d. 1983)
Antanas Račiūnas, Lithuanian composer and pedagogue, born in Užliaušiai, Lithuania (d. 1984)

Alexander Moyzes, Slovak composer, born in Kláštor pod Znievom, Slovensko (d. 1984)
Han G. Hoekstra, Dutch poet (Zandloperr; Ongerijmde life), journalist, and editor, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1988)
Leo Castelli, Italian-American art dealer, born in Trieste, Austrian Littoral, Austria-Hungary (d. 1999)
Maurice Ashley, British 17th century historian and editor of "The Listener", birthplace unknown (d. 1994)
Edward Dmytryk, Canadian film director (Carpetbaggers, Young Lions, Caine Mutiny), born in Grand Forks, Canada (d. 1999)
Richard Wright, American author (Native Son, Uncle Tom's Children), born in Roxie, Mississippi (d. 1960)
Karel Horky, Czech composer, born in Stemechy, Austrian Empire (d. 1988)
Alexander Liberman, Russian-American magazine editor, painter and photographer (639), born in Kiev, Russian Empire (d. 1999)
Kenzo Tange, Japanese architect (Kagawa Prefectural Government Office Building; Pritzker Prize, 1987), born in Sakai, Japan (d. 2005)
Mickey Cohen, American gangster (Cohen crime family), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1976)
Stanford Moore, American biochemist (Nobel 1977), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1982)
Henrik Blichmann, Danish swing jazz pianist, composer (92 Minutes of Yesterday), and orchestra leader, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1994)
Rudolf Leiding, German auto executive and 3rd postwar chairman of Volkswagen, born in Altmark, Saxony-Anhalt, Germany (d. 2003)
Dick Thomas, American singing cowboy and TV host (Village Barn), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2003)
Rudolf Schock, German opera tenor, born in Duisburg, Germany (d. 1986)
Robert A. W. Lowndes, American sci-fi author and editor of "Future Science Fiction", born in Bridgeport, Connecticut (d. 1988)
Henry Ford II, American automotive industry businessman, grandson of Henry Ford, and son of Edsel Ford (Ford Motor Company President, 1945-60 and CEO, 1947-79), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 1987)
Bill Talbert, American tennis player (9 x Grand Slam doubles titles; US Open singles 1944, 45 runner-up; International Tennis HOF), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1999)
Gerald Wilson, American jazz trumpeter, arranger (Jimmie Lunceford; Duke Ellington), orchestra leader, and teacher, born in Shelby, Mississippi (d. 2014)
Howard Morris, American comedic actor and director (The Andy Griffith Show, High Anxiety), born in New York City (d. 2005)
Craig Claiborne, American food columnist (NY Times Cookbook), born in Sunflower, Mississippi (d. 2000)
Harold "Shot" Jackson, American country dobro player, and pedal steel guitarist (Roy Acuff), and guitar designer and builder (Sho-Bud), born in Wilmington, North Carolina (d. 1991)
Teddy Johnson, British singer (Pearl Carr & Teddy Johnson), born in Surbiton, Surrey (d. 2018)
Per Olof Sundman, Swedish writer and politician, born in Vaxholm, Sweden (d. 1992)
Stan Burns, American television screenwriter (The Tonight Show, 1954-57; Get Smart; The Carol Burnet Show; The Flip Wilson Show; Dean Martin Roasts), born in Brooklyn, NYC (d. 2002)
